g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 3. Today we explore Bitcoin, the revolutionary decentralized cryptocurrency that pioneered blockchain technology.
The double-spending problem: how can digital money be spent twice without a central authority verifying transactions?
g.text: Digital files can be copied - how do we prevent someone from spending the same
digital money twice? Traditional systems use trusted central authorities (banks).

g.data-narration: Satoshi Nakamoto solved this in 2008 with Bitcoin - a decentralized blockchain using Proof-of-Work consensus.
g.text: A decentralized peer-to-peer electronic payment system using blockchain
technology and Proof-of-Work to solve double-spending without central authority.

g.data-narration: Bitcoin is a distributed peer-to-peer protocol - a set of rules that all network participants follow for creating and verifying transactions.
g.text: A distributed peer-to-peer protocol defining rules for creating, transmitting,
and verifying transactions without a central bank or administrator.

g.data-narration: The Bitcoin blockchain is a public, immutable ledger - blocks containing transactions cryptographically linked together in a chain.
g.text: A public, immutable, append-only ledger - chain of blocks with transactions.
Each block contains: Previous Hash + Timestamp + Nonce + Merkle Root + Transactions.

g.data-narration: Proof-of-Work requires miners to solve cryptographic puzzles - finding a nonce that produces a hash below a target. This secures the network.
g.text: Miners compete to solve cryptographic puzzle: find nonce such that Hash(block) less than Target.
Successful miner earns block reward (new BTC + transaction fees).

g.data-narration: Bitcoin uses public-private key cryptography. Your public address receives funds; your private key authorizes spending with a digital signature.
g.text: No accounts - ownership proven through key pairs. Private key signs transactions;
public key (address) receives funds. Signature verified by network nodes.

g.data-narration: Bitcoin supply is capped at 21 million coins. Block rewards halve every 4 years, creating digital scarcity - earning Bitcoin the nickname digital gold.
g.text: Maximum 21 million BTC. Block reward started at 50 BTC, halves every 210,000 blocks
(about 4 years). This disinflationary model mimics gold extraction - Digital Gold.

g.data-narration: A Bitcoin transaction flows through: creation, signing, broadcast, validation, mining, confirmation. Let us trace Sarah paying David 0.1 BTC.
g.text: Transaction Flow: Sarah to David (0.1 BTC)
1. Sarah creates transaction, signs with private key - 2. Broadcast to network
- 3. Nodes validate - 4. Miners add to mempool - 5. Block mined - 6. Confirmation

g.data-narration: Bitcoin pioneered trustless consensus - a breakthrough enabling decentralization without knowing or trusting other participants. This foundational concept underpins all blockchain technology.
